source_paper_title: Approximation Algorithms and Linear Programming Relaxations for Scheduling Problems Related to Min-Sum Set Cover
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Happach_2024, title={Approximation Algorithms and Linear Programming Relaxations for Scheduling Problems Related to Min-Sum Set Cover}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1368}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1368}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Happach, Felix and Schulz, Andreas S.}, year={2024}, month=feb, pages={578–598} }
context:
Section 7 (Conclusion), page 22 (arXiv v1 pagination)

Note that it is not clear whether the analyses of the algorithms in Section 2 are tight.

In view of the integrality gaps in Sections 3 and 5, it would be interesting to obtain stronger bounds on the integrality gap of the time-indexed formulation considered in Section 2.
title: Bound the integrality gap of time-indexed LP for bipartite AND/OR scheduling
background_latex:
Consider the single-machine scheduling problem with AND/OR precedence constraints. There is a set of jobs partitioned as \(N=A\uplus B\). Each job \(j\in N\) has a processing time \(p_j\ge 0\) and a weight \(w_j\ge 0\). A schedule is a non-preemptive ordering of the jobs on one machine with no overlap; \(C_j\) denotes the completion time of job \(j\). AND-precedence constraints are given by a directed arc set \(E_{\wedge}\subseteq (A\times A)\cup (B\times B)\); an arc \((i,j)\in E_{\wedge}\) requires that \(i\) completes before \(j\) starts. OR-precedence constraints are given by an arc set \(E_{\vee}\subseteq A\times B\). For each \(b\in B\), its set of OR-predecessors is \(P(b):=\{a\in A:(a,b)\in E_{\vee}\}\); feasibility requires that each \(b\in B\) with \(P(b)\neq\emptyset\) starts only after at least one job in \(P(b)\) has completed. Let \(\Delta:=\max_{b\in B}|P(b)|\).

A standard approach uses a time-indexed linear programming relaxation over a discrete time horizon \(T:=\sum_{j\in N} p_j\) (for instance in the unit-processing-time case \(p_j\in\{0,1\}\), one has \(T\le n\)). Introduce variables \(x_{jt}\) indicating (fractionally) that job \(j\) completes at integer time \(t\in\{1,\dots,T\}\). The relaxation includes: (i) assignment constraints \(\sum_{t=1}^T x_{jt}=1\) for all \(j\); (ii) single-machine capacity constraints ensuring at most one unit-processing job executes at any time; (iii) AND-precedence constraints coupling cumulative completions so that successors cannot complete too early; and (iv) OR-precedence constraints coupling cumulative completions so that a job \(b\in B\) cannot complete by time \(t+p_b\) unless at least one predecessor \(a\in P(b)\) has completed by time \(t\). The LP objective is the fractional lower bound \(\min \sum_{j\in N} w_j\sum_{t=1}^T t\,x_{jt}\), corresponding to \(\sum_j w_j C_j\) in integer schedules.

The paper analyzes rounding algorithms based on random \(\alpha\)-points for this relaxation and notes that the tightness of these analyses (equivalently, the true worst-case integrality gap of the underlying time-indexed formulation for this AND/OR structure) is not established.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine the best (smallest) constant \(g\ge 1\) such that, for every instance of \(1\mid ao\text{-}prec=A\uplus B\mid \sum_{j\in N} w_j C_j\) and for the corresponding time-indexed linear programming relaxation described above, the ratio
\[
\frac{\text{optimal objective value of an optimal integer schedule}}{\text{optimal objective value of the time-indexed LP relaxation}}
\]
is at most \(g\). Equivalently, bound the integrality gap of the time-indexed LP relaxation for this problem class (as a function of parameters such as \(\Delta\), or by a universal constant if possible), and in particular decide whether the approximation-factor analyses obtained via random \(\alpha\)-point rounding are tight in the worst case.
latex:
Consider the single-machine scheduling problem with AND/OR precedence constraints. There is a set of jobs partitioned as \(N=A\uplus B\). Each job \(j\in N\) has a processing time \(p_j\ge 0\) and a weight \(w_j\ge 0\). OR-precedence constraints are given by an arc set \(E_{\vee}\subseteq A\times B\). For each \(b\in B\), its set of OR-predecessors is \(P(b):=\{a\in A:(a,b)\in E_{\vee}\}\); feasibility requires that each \(b\in B\) with \(P(b)\neq\emptyset\) starts only after at least one job in \(P(b)\) has completed. Let \(\Delta:=\max_{b\in B}|P(b)|\).

A standard approach uses a time-indexed linear programming relaxation over a discrete time horizon \(T:=\sum_{j\in N} p_j\) (for instance in the unit-processing-time case \(p_j\in\{0,1\}\), one has \(T\le n\)). open_problem_quote:
Note that it is not clear whether the analyses of the algorithms in Section 2 are tight.

In view of the integrality gaps in Sections 3 and 5, it would be interesting to obtain stronger bounds on the integrality gap of the time-indexed formulation considered in Section 2.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For fixed sizes and bounded time horizon (e.g., unit processing times), one can generate instances and solve both the LP and the exact integer optimum (via MILP) to search for large LP gaps, but the search space over precedence graphs and weights grows quickly and worst-case gaps may require structured constructions.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate instances (graphs, processing times, weights) can be programmatically generated and scored by the computable fitness signal given by the LP integrality-gap ratio (solve LP + exact/MIP or strong bounds). An evolutionary loop can therefore search for extremal families and potentially suggest tight examples or improved conjectured bounds as a function of \(\Delta\).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
No forward-citation evidence was provided beyond the 2020 source paper, so there is currently no identified later work that resolves the integrality-gap question for the specific time-indexed LP relaxation for bipartite AND/OR precedence. In the source paper, the authors obtain \((2\Delta)\) and \((2\Delta+\varepsilon)\) approximation guarantees via random \(\alpha\)-point rounding (with different \(\alpha\) for jobs in \(A\) and \(B\)), but they explicitly state that it is unclear whether these analyses are tight, i.e. whether the underlying time-indexed/interval-indexed LP has integrality gap matching \(\Theta(\Delta)\) or perhaps a smaller constant independent of \(\Delta\).

Related foundational work on time-indexed LPs and \(\alpha\)-point scheduling for \(1\mid prec\mid \sum w_j C_j\) with AND-precedence constraints (Hall–Shmoys–Wein; Hall–Schulz–Shmoys–Wein; Schulz; Schulz–Skutella) supplies the main analytical toolkit, including the use of non-uniform randomization over \(\beta\) to control \(1/\beta\) terms. For special cases such as min-sum set cover (Feige–Lov\'asz–Tetali), LP-based methods are known to be tight up to constant factors (notably the factor 4 barrier for MSSC), suggesting that tightness questions for richer precedence structures are subtle.

The source paper also demonstrates that other natural LP formulations (linear-ordering and completion-time formulations, even strengthened with additional valid inequalities) can have integrality gaps linear in \(n\) even when \(\Delta=2\) and \(E_{\wedge}=\emptyset\). This underscores that the time-indexed LP is the most promising relaxation in the OR-precedence setting, and that proving a sharp integrality-gap bound for it likely requires either explicit gap instances tailored to the \(\alpha\)-point rounding analysis or new structural inequalities/rounding arguments that exploit the bipartite \(A\uplus B\) AND/OR form.

sources.title: Approximation Algorithms and LP Relaxations for Scheduling Problems Related to Min-Sum Set Cover
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the bipartite AND/OR single-machine problem \(1\mid ao\text{-}prec=A\uplus B\mid \sum_j w_j C_j\), proposes time-indexed and interval-indexed LP relaxations, and gives \((2\Delta)\)-approximation for \(p_j\in\{0,1\}\) and \((2\Delta+\varepsilon)\)-approximation for general \(p_j\) via random \(\alpha\)-point rounding with different \(\alpha\) for \(A\) and \(B\). It explicitly notes that tight integrality-gap bounds for the time-indexed LP analysis are unclear.
sources.connection: This is the source paper that formulates the open question: determine the true worst-case integrality gap of the time-indexed LP (Sousa–Wolsey style with added OR constraints) for bipartite AND/OR precedence, and whether the \(2\Delta\) (or \(2\Delta+\varepsilon\)) analysis is tight.

sources.title: Approximating min-sum set cover
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives a 4-approximation for min-sum set cover using a time-indexed LP and a primal-dual style analysis; establishes the LP as a natural relaxation for min-sum covering objectives.
sources.connection: MSSC is a special case of the bipartite OR part of the AND/OR scheduling model (with \(p_a=1, w_b=1, p_b=0, w_a=0\)). The time-indexed LP techniques and \(\alpha\)-point style reasoning motivate the LP under study, but do not resolve its integrality gap for the richer AND/OR precedence structure.

sources.title: Approximating min sum set cover
sources.theorem_or_result: Journal version: 4-approximation for MSSC and hardness of improving the factor below 4; clarifies the LP-based analysis and tightness for MSSC.
sources.connection: Provides a benchmark where the natural time-indexed LP and rounding analysis is essentially tight (factor 4) for MSSC. This informs expectations about tightness of \(\alpha\)-point analyses, but does not directly bound the integrality gap for bipartite AND/OR scheduling with \(\Delta\) and AND arcs.

sources.title: Scheduling to minimize average completion time: Off-line and on-line algorithms
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces \(\alpha\)-point scheduling from time-indexed LPs for precedence-constrained single-machine \(\sum w_j C_j\), obtaining constant-factor approximations (notably \(4+\varepsilon\) in early forms) for AND-precedence constraints.
sources.connection: The Happach–Schulz rounding for AND/OR constraints is an adaptation of this \(\alpha\)-point framework. Understanding integrality gaps for the AND/OR time-indexed LP builds on the same LP machinery developed here.

sources.title: Scheduling to minimize average completion time: Off-line and on-line approximation algorithms
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops LP-based \(\alpha\)-point rounding for \(1\mid prec\mid \sum w_j C_j\) with AND-precedence constraints, giving \(4\)-type guarantees and foundational analysis tools for time-indexed/interval-indexed relaxations.
sources.connection: Serves as the canonical reference for time-indexed LP relaxations and \(\alpha\)-point rounding in \(\sum w_j C_j\) scheduling. The open problem asks for the integrality gap of an analogous LP once OR-precedence constraints are added in the bipartite AND/OR structure.

sources.title: Scheduling to minimize total weighted completion time: Performance guarantees of LP-based heuristics and lower bounds
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes performance guarantees for LP-based list scheduling for \(\sum w_j C_j\) under AND-precedence constraints and develops lower-bound techniques for LP relaxations in scheduling.
sources.connection: Provides foundational LP-gap/rounding analysis techniques for \(\sum w_j C_j\) scheduling that underpin later \(\alpha\)-point methods; relevant as methodological background for analyzing the integrality gap of the AND/OR time-indexed LP.

sources.title: Random-based scheduling: New approximations and LP lower bounds
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops randomized \(\alpha\)-point scheduling analyses and LP lower bounds for single-machine scheduling variants, including techniques to control \(1/\beta\) terms via non-uniform distributions over \(\beta\).
sources.connection: Happach–Schulz use the same idea (density \(f(\beta)=2\beta\)) to cancel \(1/\beta\) blowups in expected completion times. These techniques are central to any attempt to sharpen the integrality-gap bound for the AND/OR time-indexed LP.

sources.title: Precedence-constrained min sum set cover
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies MSSC with AND-precedence constraints on the elements/vertices; gives a \(4\sqrt{|V|}\)-approximation and discusses hardness via the planted dense subgraph conjecture.
sources.connection: This is a related AND+OR-flavored covering/scheduling setting. It motivates parameterizations (e.g. by \(\Delta\)) and suggests that strong lower bounds may depend on conjectures. It does not directly bound the integrality gap of the specific time-indexed LP for bipartite AND/OR scheduling.

sources.title: Scheduling AND/OR-networks on identical parallel machines
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows strong inapproximability for scheduling with general AND/OR precedence structures (multiple layers), and gives simple approximation bounds for some variants.
sources.connection: Provides hardness context: once general AND/OR precedence is allowed, constant-factor approximations may be impossible. The open problem focuses on the restricted bipartite AND/OR structure \(A\uplus B\) and the integrality gap of a specific time-indexed LP in that setting.
